sections.heading: AWS IAM Authentication (Federated Database Instance)
sections.body:
Use an AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) User or Role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) to authenticate a
database user. Using AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) reduces the number of authentication mechanisms and
number of secrets to manage. Atlas does not receive your authentication secret key
over the wire and the driver does not persist it.

> **Note:** Atlas uses AWS (Amazon Web Services) STS (Security Token Service) to verify the
> identity of IAM (Identity and Access Management) users and roles. AWS (Amazon Web Services) enforces a [default request
> quota](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_iam-quotas.html)
> of 600 requests per second, per account, per region. This quota is
> applied against the AWS (Amazon Web Services) account of the IAM (Identity and Access Management) user or role.

sections.heading: Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
You can set up AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) Roles to authenticate AWS (Amazon Web Services) compute types to your Atlas
clusters.

> **Note:** You can't set up authentication for AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM principals when LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization is
> enabled.
>
> If you require authentication for an AWS IAM principal, consider moving the
> clusters that you want to access with AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM authentication into another project
> where LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization is disabled.

For AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda and HTTP (ECS and EC2), drivers automatically read from the
[environment variables](https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/auth/auth.rst#obtaining-credentials). For AWS (Amazon Web Services) EKS, you
must manually assign the IAM (Identity and Access Management) role.

This page describes how AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda, AWS (Amazon Web Services) ECS (Elastic Container Service), and
AWS (Amazon Web Services) EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) can connect using an AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role.

> **Note:** You must assign an IAM (Identity and Access Management) role to Lambda, EC2, ECS, or EKS in the
> AWS (Amazon Web Services) console.

AWS Lambda

AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda passes information to functions through the following
environment variables if you [assign an execution role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-intro-execution-role.html) to the
lambda function.

- `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`

- `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`

- `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN`

> **Note:** You don't need to manually create these environment variables when
> you use an [execution role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-intro-execution-role.html) in your
> function.

To learn more about these environment variables, see [Using AWS Lambda
environment variables](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-envvars.html).

AWS ECS

AWS (Amazon Web Services) ECS (Elastic Container Service) gets the credentials from
the following URI:

```shell
http://169.254.170.2${AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI}
```

`AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI` is an environment variable.
To learn more, see [IAM Roles for Tasks](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-iam-roles.html) in the AWS documentation.

AWS (Amazon Web Services) EC2 gets the credentials from Instance Metadata Service V2 at the
following URL (Uniform Resource Locator):

```shell
http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
```

To learn more, see [Launch an instance with an IAM role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/iam-roles-for-amazon-ec2.html#attach-iam-role) in the AWS
documentation.

AWS ECS Fargate

To learn how to configure an AWS IAM role for authentication with
AWS (Amazon Web Services) ECS Fargate, see the [Amazon ECS task execution IAM role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html) in the AWS documentation.

AWS EKS

For AWS (Amazon Web Services) EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service), you must first
assign the IAM (Identity and Access Management) role to your pod to set up the following environment
variables in that pod:

- `AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE` - contains the path to the web
  identity token file.

- `AWS_ROLE_ARN` - contains the IAM (Identity and Access Management) role used to connect to
  your cluster.

To learn more about AWS EKS, see [What is Amazon EKS?](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/what-is-eks.html) in the AWS documentation.

sections.heading: Grant Database Access to AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
To grant database access to the AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role, complete the steps
described in the [Configure Database Users](mongodb-users) section for AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management). For
more information on granting database access using Atlas CLI,
Atlas Administration API, or Atlas UI, see [Configure Database Users](add-mongodb-users).

sections.heading: Connect to Atlas Cluster Using AWS IAM
sections.body:
To connect to Atlas with your AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) credentials using
[mongosh](mongodb-shell), provide [a connection string that specifies the MONGODB-AWS authentication mechanism](manual/reference/connection-string). This connection string
format applies to all AWS IAM authentication mechanisms.

> **Important:** You must configure authentication using one of the
> methods described in [Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles](passwordless-auth-aws-no-saml) before
> you can use this connection string format.

Connecting to Atlas using AWS IAM authentication with the
[mongosh](mongodb-shell) requires shell version v0.9.0 or higher.

Consider the following:

- Use your [AWS IAM credentials](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/home?region=us-west-2#/security_credentials),
  using your access key ID as your username and your secret key as your
  password.

- The `authSource` query parameter is `$external`, URL-encoded as
  `%24external`.

- The `authMechanism` query parameter is `MONGODB-AWS`.

  > **Example:** 
  >
  > ```sh
  > mongosh "mongodb+srv://<atlas-host-name>/test?authSource=%24external&authMechanism=MONGODB-AWS" --username <access-key-id> --password <secret-key>
  > ```

> **See Also:** [IAM roles for service accounts](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/iam-roles-for-service-accounts.html)

sections.heading: X509 Authentication (Federated Database Instance)
sections.body:
X.509 certificates provide [database users](manual/reference/glossary) access to the
clusters in their project. Note that Database users are different from [Atlas users](manual/reference/glossary). Database users have access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas
users have access to the Atlas application itself.

X.509 certificates can be of one of the following types:

- **Self-managed certificates**. With self-managed certificates, you must provide a
  Certificate Authority (CA) and generate your own certificates for your database users. See
  [Configure a Project to use a Public Key Infrastructure](configure-pki) for more details on setting up self-managed certificates in your
  project.

- **Atlas-managed certificates**. With Atlas-managed certificates, Atlas manages your
  CA as well and generates the certificates for your database users. You do not need to
  handle any additional CA configuration with Atlas-managed certificates.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To self-manage your X.509 certificates, you must have a [Public Key Infrastructure
(PKI)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_key_infrastructure) to integrate with Atlas.

sections.heading: Configure a Project to use a Public Key Infrastructure
sections.body:
If you choose to work with self-managed X.509 certificates, you must configure your
project to use a PKI you provide.

Toggle Self-Managed X.509 Authentication to
ON.

Atlas CLI

To save one customer-managed X.509 configuration for the project you specify using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas security customerCerts create [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas security customerCerts create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-security-customerCerts-create).

Atlas UI

You can provide a Certificate Authority (CA) using the
Atlas UI by either:

- Clicking Upload and selecting a `.pem` file from
  your filesystem and clicking Save.

- Copying the contents of a `.pem` file into the provided text
  area and clicking Save.

You can concatenate multiple CAs in the same `.pem` file or
in the text area. Users can authenticate with certificates
generated by any of the provided CAs.

When you upload a CA, a project-level alert is
automatically created to send a notification 30 days before
the CA expires, repeating every 24 hours. You can view and
edit this alert from Atlas's Alert Settings page. For more information on configuring alerts, see
Configure Alert Settings.

To edit your CA once uploaded, click the
**Self-Managed X.509 Authentication Settings**
pencil-alt icon.

sections.heading: Add a Database User using Self-Managed X.509 Authentication
sections.body:
- If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Add New Database User.

| Field           | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| --------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Common Name     | The user's Common Name (CN) protected by the TLS/SSL certificate. For more information, see RFC 2253.For example, if your common name is "Jane Doe", your organization is "MongoDB", and your country is "US", insert the following into the Common Name field:CN=Jane Doe,O=MongoDB,C=US                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| User Privileges | You can assign roles in one of the following ways:Select Atlas admin, which provides the user with readWriteAnyDatabase as well as a number of administrative privileges.Select readWriteAnyDatabase, which provides the user with privileges to read and write to any database.Select readAnyDatabase which provides the user with privileges to read any database.Select Select Custom Role to select a custom role previously created in Atlas. You can create custom roles for database users in cases where the built-in database user roles cannot describe the desired set of privileges. For more information on custom roles, see Configure Custom Database Roles.Click Add Default Privileges. When you click this option, you can select individual roles and specify the database on which the roles apply. Optionally, for the read and readWrite roles, you can also specify a collection. If you do not specify a collection for read and readWrite, the role applies to all non-system collections in the database.For more information on the built-in Atlas privileges, see Built-In Roles.For more information on authorization, see Role-Based Access Control and Built-in Roles in the MongoDB manual. |
